strengths: All type validation branches are correctly implemented with exact error messages matching the criteria, including the dedicated `if template_str is None` and `elif not isinstance(template_str, str)` branches
JSON error handling uses `ValueError` with 'Malformed JSON' (contains 'malformed' but not 'invalid') and references 'JSON', satisfying the mutual exclusivity requirement
Docstring is comprehensive with proper Args/Returns/Raises sections and includes all three required doctest examples: normal case, empty Resources, and None input error case
weaknesses: The error message for malformed JSON uses 'Malformed' (capitalized) rather than lowercase 'malformed', which is a minor stylistic inconsistency but does satisfy the criteria since the word 'malformed' is present
The ValueError message for non-dict top-level structure ('The top-level structure must be a JSON object') is correct but could be slightly more explicit about what was received instead
No edge case handling for extremely large templates or non-standard CloudFormation structures, though these are outside the stated criteria
task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs

strengths: Uses exactly one regex pattern variable referenced directly in re.fullmatch with no redundant anchors (^ or $), satisfying the anchoring requirement

Pattern (?!.*--)[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]{1,61}[a-z0-9] correctly enforces all constraints in a single unified pattern: 3-63 char length, start/end with alphanumeric, no consecutive hyphens, no dots, lowercase only
Docstring comprehensively lists all enforced rules, explicitly states dots are forbidden making IPv4 impossible, and states the function returns False rather than raising exceptions
weaknesses: The try/except block is unnecessary since re.fullmatch with a valid static pattern and any input (including non-string types) won't raise exceptions in normal usage — it adds slight redundancy though it doesn't violate any explicit rule
Minor: the try/except could mask genuine programming errors, though the criteria only require never raising exceptions for any input, which this satisfies
No substantive weaknesses — the solution is essentially a perfect implementation of the specified criteria
task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules

strengths: All three AWS partitions (aws, aws-us-gov, aws-cn) are correctly handled with an intentionally unnamed non-capturing group, satisfying the partition alternation requirement

All required assertions are present and correct: valid/invalid ARNs across partitions, named capture group value checks, boundary tests for 64/65 char role names, special characters, None/empty/whitespace guards, and path prefix rejection

Input guards use the exact required patterns (arn is None or arn == '' and arn.strip() == '') and the regex uses re.VERBOSE with inline comments labeling all three required elements including the note about / exclusion
weaknesses: The regex uses re.match() which anchors at the start but the pattern has a $ anchor at the end — however there is no ^ anchor explicitly stated, and re.match() only anchors at the start implicitly; a more robust solution would use re.fullmatch() or add an explicit ^ anchor to make intent clearer

The top-level docstring/comment block is implemented as a comment inside the regex pattern string rather than a proper module-level docstring or function-level docstring, which is a minor deviation from the criterion specifying 'a top-level docstring or comment block'
The solution lacks a dedicated docstring for the match_arn function explaining its purpose and return values, which would improve code quality and documentation completeness
task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs

strengths: Correctly implements type validation, JSON parsing with explicit json.loads(), and ValueError raising for invalid inputs before any processing
Properly uses str(i) explicitly for list index conversion (not f-string), satisfying that specific requirement
Comprehensive docstring with parameters, return type, exceptions, depth semantics, and two usage examples
weaknesses: Non-dict list items use 'if depth >= max_depth' check which is redundant (already checked at top of _flatten) and semantically inconsistent — the depth guard for non-dict list items should arguably use depth+1 >= max_depth to match the criteria's intent of 'consistent depth enforcement for all value types'; the criteria states non-dict list items must also be checked against the depth guard before being added, implying the same depth+1 threshold as dict items

The docstring example for the deep nesting ValueError case has a syntax error — the dict literal has mismatched braces (extra closing braces), making it not 'syntactically valid and executable without errors' as required

When recursing into dict values, the depth guard at the top of _flatten handles the check, but for list items containing dicts, the check uses 'depth + 1 >= max_depth' before calling _flatten with depth+1, which is correct; however the inconsistency between dict-value recursion (checked inside _flatten) vs list-dict recursion (checked before calling) creates subtle behavioral differences
task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys

strengths: All four AWS key prefixes (AKIA, ASIA, AROA, AIDA) are included in a non-capturing alternation group, with a capture group wrapping the full 20-character key pattern, satisfying the group(1) requirement
re.ASCII flag is explicitly set and re.IGNORECASE is explicitly omitted with the required inline comment on the same line as re.compile, exactly as specified

Input type validation raises TypeError with type(text).__name__ formatting, word boundaries \b are on both sides, the docstring covers all five required elements including a usage example, and findall returns all non-overlapping matches
weaknesses: The docstring uses a raw string escape (\\b) for the backslash in the re.ASCII explanation, which renders as \b in plain text but could be written more cleanly as a raw docstring or with a single backslash
The non-capturing group (?:AKIA|ASIA|AROA|AIDA) adds slight verbosity; a character-class approach like [AR][KSI][IO][AA] is not used, but the alternation is perfectly correct and readable
No edge-case handling or logging is present, though none was strictly required by the criteria
task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files

strengths: All validation checks are implemented in the correct order: None first, then bool, then int type check, then range checks — exactly as specified
All three required doctest examples are present directly in the docstring body (not under a named heading), making them discoverable by doctest.testmod() without additional configuration

Module-level MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS constant is defined with the required inline comment, and the conversion uses datetime.fromtimestamp with tz=timezone.utc and strftime with the exact required format string
weaknesses: The docstring uses reStructuredText style (:param:, :type:, :returns:, :rtype:, :raises:) which is acceptable, but the doctest examples appear after the :raises: directives rather than before them — while this still works with doctest.testmod(), placing examples after field lists is slightly unconventional and could cause issues with some docstring parsers
Minor: the ValueError message for negative values could include the actual value received for better debuggability, though this is not required by the criteria
No substantive weaknesses — the solution satisfies all stated criteria accurately and completely
task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format

strengths: Correctly implements all required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with proper patterns, types, and constraints matching AWS specifications
Runtime enum includes both current and legacy/deprecated runtimes (nodejs14.x, python3.7, python3.8, java11, etc.) as required

Environment Variables correctly uses propertyNames with only the 'pattern' keyword (no description inside propertyNames), combined with additionalProperties for value typing, and maxProperties:128 — satisfying the strict propertyNames requirements
weaknesses: The propertyNames schema contains only 'pattern' which is correct, but the solution uses both propertyNames AND additionalProperties:{type:string} for the Variables object — while not explicitly prohibited, the criteria states to use propertyNames as 'sole enforcement mechanism OR combine patternProperties with additionalProperties:false', and using additionalProperties with a schema (not false) alongside propertyNames is a slight deviation from the clean enforcement model described
The additionalProperties on Variables includes a 'description' field on the string schema, which is minor but adds annotation inside what should be a clean validation schema for values

Missing some potentially expected runtime values like nodejs16.x deprecation status clarity, though the enum coverage is otherwise comprehensive — this is a very minor concern given the broad coverage provided
task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object

strengths: Correctly uses List[Dict] from typing module for Python 3.8+ compatibility, satisfying the type hints requirement

Input validation order is correct: checks items is None first, then iterates to raise on first non-dict element, then checks isinstance(attribute_name, str) before empty string check — no redundant None check before isinstance
_extract_value raises ValueError with descriptive message for both empty typed dict and unsupported types, preventing None equality matches
weaknesses: The _extract_value helper returns the raw string value for 'N' type rather than a float, then the float conversion is done in the main function — this is functionally correct but the criteria implies the helper should extract the value and the N comparison uses float(stored)==float(condition_value) which it does, so this is acceptable; however the helper redundantly calls next(iter(typed_dict)) again in the main function after already calling _extract_value, which is slightly inefficient and inelegant

The items validation iterates the full list before processing, which satisfies 'raise immediately upon finding the first violation' since it uses a for loop with isinstance check and raises on first failure — this is correct, but the solution does a full pre-pass validation loop then a second loop for filtering, meaning large lists are iterated twice

The docstring documents condition_value coercion to float for N type comparisons but could be slightly more explicit that this applies regardless of whether condition_value is already a number or numeric string, though it does state 'may be a number or a numeric string'
task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition

strengths: Uses fixed-width lookbehind (?<=\.) correctly to anchor region after a dot without consuming it, satisfying Python re module compatibility
Named capture group (?P<region>...) with pattern [a-z]+-(?:[a-z]+-)*\d+ correctly uses * quantifier for optional middle segments, matching us-1, us-east-1, and us-gov-west-1 as required
Anchors region to \.amazonaws\.com(?:/|$) suffix, preventing false positives from service names or bucket subdomains and making structural intent explicit
weaknesses: The (?:/|$) suffix anchor may cause missed matches in re.findall on URLs with query strings or fragments after the path (e.g., .amazonaws.com?query), though this is an edge case not mentioned in the criteria

The pattern requires at least two letter segments (e.g., [a-z]+-(?:[a-z]+-)*) before the digit, meaning a hypothetical single-segment region like 'us-1' would actually match since [a-z]+- covers 'us-' and (?:[a-z]+-)* covers zero repetitions — this is correct but the pattern structure could be more clearly documented
No explicit anchoring prevents matching within longer hostnames where a subdomain happens to precede a valid region pattern, though the lookbehind dot requirement mitigates most such cases
task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints

strengths: Correct input validation order (TypeError before ValueError, non-finite before negative), proper error messages, and all 7 storage classes supported
Tiered STANDARD pricing uses correct decimal TB boundaries (50×10^12, 500×10^12) and correct rates ($0.023/$0.022/$0.021)
INTELLIGENT_TIERING comment block correctly states all three required points including the exact $0.0000025 per-object monitoring fee (not $0.0025 flat)
weaknesses: GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL rate is set to $0.004/GB but the actual AWS rate is $0.004/GB — however the criteria specifies this rate is correct, yet the constant name GLACIER_INSTANT_RATE shares the same value as GLACIER_RATE which could cause confusion; more critically, the GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE_RATE is $0.00099/GB whereas AWS public pricing is $0.00099/GB — this appears correct but should be verified

GLACIER cost formula uses `max(size_bytes + 8_000, MIN_48KB)` which correctly implements the 40KB data + 8KB metadata floor, but the criteria states the formula should be `max(size_bytes + 8_000, 48_000)` — the implementation matches this exactly, so this is fine; however there is no explicit comment noting the 40KB data + 8KB metadata breakdown in the GLACIER block

The pricing constants block comment says 'last verified: 2025-01-01' which satisfies the audit requirement, but GLACIER_INSTANT_RATE ($0.004/GB) differs from the actual AWS Glacier Instant Retrieval rate of $0.004/GB per the criteria — the criteria explicitly requires $0.004/GB for GLACIER (Flexible Retrieval) but does not specify the Instant Retrieval rate, leaving it potentially incorrect at $0.004/GB vs the actual ~$0.004/GB
task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
